title: How can India be a top-five economy and still one of the poorest?
tension: The same country is an economic giant and, person by person, near the bottom. Add everything India produces and it is one of the five largest economies on earth, courted at every global summit. Divide that same output across 1.4 billion people and the average Indian is poorer than the average Bangladeshi by one common measure, and a fraction of a Chinese or South Korean. Both facts are true at once. The whole story of India sits in the gap between the total and the average, and even the average lies, because it is not what most people earn.
teachingGoal: Resolve the paradox a reader meets constantly: India is simultaneously huge and poor. They should understand why the global ranking flips between PPP and market exchange rates, why dividing by population collapses the picture, why even per-capita is an average that sits above what most people earn, and the difference between output produced here (GDP) and income owned by Indians (GNI).
sectionArc: How big is India's economy next to the giants? (market-exchange-rate dollars against Japan, Germany, the UK, China, the US)
Is India the fifth-largest or the third? (PPP vs market exchange rate; why the ranking flips; what PPP adjusts for)
So why does a top-five economy feel poor? (divide by 1.4 billion; per-capita against Bangladesh, Vietnam, Indonesia, China, South Korea)
Is per-capita income what people actually earn? (it is GDP divided by population, an arithmetic average, not a wage; inequality pulls the average above what most earn)
Produced in India, or owned by Indians? (GDP vs GNI; net income paid abroad)
What does 'big but poor' actually decide for India? (the weight of a large economy in the world versus the thin resources per person at home)
mustTeach: by total size at market exchange rates India is among the world's five largest economies
by purchasing-power parity India ranks higher (third) because a rupee buys more in India than a dollar buys in the US
per-capita income reverses the ranking: India is poor per person, comparable to Bangladesh and far below China or South Korea
per-capita GDP is an arithmetic average (GDP / population), not a salary, and inequality means most people earn less than it
GDP measures production inside the borders; GNI measures income accruing to residents, and the two differ by net income paid abroad
featureNumbers: India GDP at market exchange rate, latest
India GDP at purchasing-power parity, latest
United Kingdom — GDP (market exchange rate), latest
India — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
Bangladesh — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
China — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
South Korea — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
Indonesia — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
Vietnam — GDP per capita (market exchange rate), latest
Net primary income paid abroad (GDP − GNI), latest

contextCards.fact: Purchasing-power parity (PPP) adjusts for the fact that the same money buys much more in India than in a rich country: a haircut, a meal, a bus ride, a month's rent all cost a fraction of what they cost in the US. So India's GDP measured at PPP is far larger than at the market exchange rate, and the global ranking rises. Market-rate dollars answer 'what is the output worth in dollars'; PPP answers 'how much can it actually buy'. Both are correct; they answer different questions.
